original_abstract:
In the context of high-dimensional financial data, feature selection is a critical preprocessing step for optimizing the performance and efficiency of machine learning models. This study presents a comprehensive comparative analysis of two prominent feature selection strategies: SHapley Additive exPlanations (SHAP) values and models' built-in feature importance mechanisms. Utilizing the Kaggle Credit Card Fraud Detection Dataset, we evaluate these techniques across five diverse classifiers—XGBoost, CatBoost, Random Forest, Extremely Randomized Trees, and Decision Trees—using various feature subset sizes. Performance is measured using the Area Under the Precision-Recall Curve (AUPRC), a robust metric for the highly imbalanced nature of fraud detection data. 

Our experimental results and subsequent statistical analyses (ANOVA and Tukey’s HSD tests) demonstrate that, overall, traditional importance-based selection methods significantly outperform SHAP-based selection across most learners and subset sizes. While SHAP offers high interpretability rooted in game theory, it incurs substantial computational costs and provides a lower return on investment regarding classification accuracy compared to built-in importance rankings. Furthermore, we identify optimal feature subset sizes and rank the classifiers, finding that Random Forest and XGBoost paired with built-in importance consistently deliver superior results. Given the computational complexity of SHAP, our findings suggest that utilizing a model's inherent feature importance is a more practical and efficient strategy for large-scale, high-dimensional datasets in the fraud detection domain.
humanized_abstract:
Feature selection is one of the methods to preprocess high-dimensional data so that machine learning models can achieve the best performance and speed. In this work, we explored a range of methods that can be used for feature selection and we compare two prominent approaches namely SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations) values and feature importance of a model. Our work utilized the Kaggle Credit Card Fraud Detection Dataset, where we evaluated the performance of five different classifiers such as XGBoost, CatBoost, Random Forest, Extremely Randomized Trees, Decision Trees. A wide variety of SHAP values and model feature importance were obtained by taking different subsets of features from the full dataset and results are compared based on Area Under the Precision-Recall Curve (AUPRC), a suitable evaluation metric for imbalanced datasets such as the Credit Card Fraud Detection Dataset.

Experimental results with accompanying statistical analysis (using ANOVA and Tukey’s HSD tests) show that, for most learners and subset sizes, traditional importance-based selection methods lead to better classification performance than SHAP-based selection. SHAP has the advantage of highly interpretable explanations based on game theory, but it is expensive to compute and provides a lower return on investment in terms of model accuracy than the default importance metrics for all learners. We also determine the optimal subset sizes for each learner and obtain the rankings of the different learners. Random Forest and XGBoost with default importance metrics provide the best results. Due to the high cost of SHAP, we find that using the default importance metrics provided by the models leads to better results for larger and higher dimensional datasets, which is common in fraud detection.

document.output:
Feature selection is one of the methods to preprocess high-dimensional data so that machine learning models can achieve the best performance and speed. In this work, we explored a range of methods that can be used for feature selection and we compare two prominent approaches namely SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations) values and feature importance of a model. A wide variety of SHAP values and model feature importance were obtained by taking different subsets of features from the full dataset and results are compared based on Area Under the Precision-Recall Curve (AUPRC), a suitable evaluation metric for imbalanced datasets such as the Credit Card Fraud Detection Dataset.

Experimental results with accompanying statistical analysis (using ANOVA and Tukey’s HSD tests) show that, for most learners and subset sizes, traditional importance-based selection methods lead to better classification performance than SHAP-based selection. SHAP has the advantage of highly interpretable explanations based on game theory, but it is expensive to compute and provides a lower return on investment in terms of model accuracy than the default importance metrics for all learners. We also determine the optimal subset sizes for each learner and obtain the rankings of the different learners. Random Forest and XGBoost with default importance metrics provide the best results. Due to the high cost of SHAP, we find that using the default importance metrics provided by the models leads to better results for larger and higher dimensional datasets, which is common in fraud detection.
document.input:
In the context of high-dimensional financial data, feature selection is a critical preprocessing step for optimizing the performance and efficiency of machine learning models. This study presents a comprehensive comparative analysis of two prominent feature selection strategies: SHapley Additive exPlanations (SHAP) values and models' built-in feature importance mechanisms. Utilizing the Kaggle Credit Card Fraud Detection Dataset, we evaluate these techniques across five diverse classifiers—XGBoost, CatBoost, Random Forest, Extremely Randomized Trees, and Decision Trees—using various feature subset sizes. Our experimental results and subsequent statistical analyses (ANOVA and Tukey’s HSD tests) demonstrate that, overall, traditional importance-based selection methods significantly outperform SHAP-based selection across most learners and subset sizes. While SHAP offers high interpretability rooted in game theory, it incurs substantial computational costs and provides a lower return on investment regarding classification accuracy compared to built-in importance rankings. Given the computational complexity of SHAP, our findings suggest that utilizing a model's inherent feature importance is a more practical and efficient strategy for large-scale, high-dimensional datasets in the fraud detection domain.
